Title: Indole-3-acetaldehyde dehydrogenase-dependent auxin synthesis contributes to virulence of Pseudomonas syringae strain DC3000

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Indole-3-acetic acid (IAA, auxin) biosynthesis via an indole-3-acetaldehyde (IAAld) intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Targeting aldA in P. syringae could reduce pathogen-produced auxin and attenuate virulence; useful as an anti-virulence target rather than a crop metabolic-engineering target. Gene editing or chemical inhibition of AldA should be considered in disease-control strategies, with caution that residual IAA production may persist through minor alternative activities and that host-derived auxin can still influence disease outcomes.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes indole-3-acetaldehyde (IAAld) to indole-3-acetic acid (IAA) using NAD+ as the preferred cofactor.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Major positive determinant of IAA accumulation in DC3000 and promotes bacterial virulence on Arabidopsis by suppressing salicylic-acid-mediated defenses.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: IAA precursor feeding; heterologous expression in E. coli with LC-MS/MS quantification of IAA; purified-protein enzyme assays and kinetics; aldA disruption mutant; complementation with wild-type genomic clone; X-ray crystallography; Arabidopsis infection assays; PR1 expression analysis.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Growth in Hoitink-Sinden minimal medium with citrate at 28°C; supplementation with Trp or IAAld; Arabidopsis infection context; SA-deficient sid2-2 host background affected virulence interpretation.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not reported; functional activity detected in bacterial culture and during plant infection.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Not reported; functional activity detected in P. syringae pv. tomato DC3000.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: AldA is the dominant IAAld dehydrogenase among the six candidate aldehyde dehydrogenases tested. Crystal structures were solved for apo AldA, AldA·NAD+, and AldA·NAD+·IAA (PDB: 5IUU; 5IUV; 5IUW). Structural analysis identified catalytic Cys302 and a hydrophobic substrate pocket suited for IAAld. Authors propose upstream formation of IAAld from Trp through an unidentified TSO activity.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: IAAld oxidation branch of tryptophan-dependent auxin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: aldA disruption reduced but did not abolish IAA production, indicating additional minor routes/enzymes. Loss of aldA reduced virulence in planta but did not affect growth in rich or defined culture, suggesting pathogen fitness cost is host-context specific.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Aldehyde dehydrogenase (ALDH) superfamily; Rossmann-fold NAD+-binding domain; catalytic cysteine-containing ALDH fold
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for DC3000 IAA production from IAAld; principal enzyme for the terminal conversion in this study
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. aldA/PSPTO_0092 was identified from candidate aldehyde dehydrogenases as the strongest catalyst converting IAAld to IAA in heterologous assays. 2. Purified AldA showed far higher specific activity and catalytic efficiency toward IAAld than AldB or AldC, with strong NAD+ preference. 3. aldA disruption reduced IAA accumulation by ~70–80% in IAAld-fed cultures, and complementation restored the phenotype. 4. Structural data explained substrate specificity and supported assignment as an IAAld dehydrogenase. 5. The aldA mutant showed reduced virulence on Arabidopsis, elevated PR1 expression, and rescue of growth on sid2-2 plants, linking pathogen-derived IAA to suppression of SA defenses.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Bacterial culture supernatant; likely plant apoplast/infected leaf tissue during pathogenesis (inferred, not directly quantified separately from host IAA)
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes P. syringae virulence by suppressing salicylic-acid-mediated host defenses; more broadly regulates plant growth and development.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: IPTG-inducible pET21a/pET28a expression system in E. coli for functional assays; native bacterial locus disrupted in DC3000
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Indole-3-acetic acid (IAA, auxin) biosynthesis via an indole-3-acetaldehyde (IAAld) intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: aldB is a weak auxiliary target for anti-virulence engineering, but its contribution to IAA synthesis is small and its role may extend beyond auxin metabolism; therefore, manipulating aldB alone is unlikely to strongly suppress pathogen-derived IAA.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Can oxidize indole-3-acetaldehyde (IAAld) to indole-3-acetic acid (IAA), but with much lower activity than AldA.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Minor contributor to IAA production in culture and contributes to virulence, although its in planta role may not be explained solely by IAA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in E. coli with LC-MS/MS IAA quantification; purified-protein enzyme assay/kinetics; aldB disruption mutant; double mutant analysis; Arabidopsis infection assays; PR1 expression analysis.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product, but likely weakly in the studied context
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Hoitink-Sinden minimal medium with IAAld supplementation; Arabidopsis infection; SA-deficient sid2-2 background used to assess defense dependence.
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Aldehyde dehydrogenase with weak indole-3-acetaldehyde dehydrogenase activity
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: In E. coli assays aldB increased IAA accumulation ~10-fold over vector control, but purified AldB had much lower specific activity and catalytic efficiency than AldA. The authors suggest AldB may prefer another aldehyde substrate in vivo, despite a small effect on IAA levels and virulence.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: IAAld oxidation branch of tryptophan-dependent auxin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: aldB mutation caused only modest or inconsistently significant reduction in IAA accumulation, yet reduced virulence; partial rescue on sid2-2 suggests additional SA-independent function(s), complicating straightforward metabolic interpretation.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established; minor auxiliary step/activity compared with AldA
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. aldB/PSPTO_2673 was one of three candidate ALDs able to convert IAAld to IAA when expressed in E. coli. 2. Purified AldB had much lower activity with IAAld than AldA, indicating it is not the principal IAAld dehydrogenase. 3. The aldB mutant showed only a small (~10–15%) reduction in IAA accumulation in culture, often weaker than aldA. 4. Despite its weak biochemical activity toward IAAld, aldB mutation reduced virulence on Arabidopsis and was only partially rescued on sid2-2 plants, implying roles beyond or in addition to SA-defense suppression through IAA.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Bacterial culture supernatant; possible contribution during infected leaf colonization
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: In the pathogen context, IAA contributes to virulence and host defense suppression.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: IPTG-inducible pET21a/pET28a expression system in E. coli for functional assays
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Putative IAAld-to-IAA oxidation within auxin biosynthesis, but not supported as biologically relevant in DC3000
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Low priority target; not supported as a meaningful determinant of IAA biosynthesis or virulence in this study.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Shows weak ability to oxidize indole-3-acetaldehyde (IAAld) to indole-3-acetic acid (IAA) in heterologous assays, but activity is very low and no in vivo contribution was detected.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Not detectably involved in DC3000 IAA synthesis in the mutant analysis; weak in vitro activity only.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in E. coli with LC-MS/MS IAA quantification; purified-protein enzyme assay/kinetics; aldC disruption mutant.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Potential direct formation of target product, but not supported as relevant in vivo
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: IAAld supplementation in bacterial culture for functional assays.
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Aldehyde dehydrogenase with very weak indole-3-acetaldehyde dehydrogenase activity
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: AldC increased IAA in E. coli only modestly and had the lowest catalytic efficiency among AldA-C. Mutation of aldC did not reduce IAA production in DC3000, so the paper concludes AldC does not appear to be involved in IAA synthesis under the tested conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Because aldC lacks a measurable in vivo role in IAA production in this system, manipulating it is unlikely to affect pathogen auxin output; its native substrate/function may lie elsewhere in metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Indole-3-acetic acid (IAA), weakly in assay systems
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. aldC/PSPTO_3644 was identified as a putative ALD able to produce a small increase in IAA from IAAld when expressed in E. coli. 2. Purified AldC displayed very low specific activity and catalytic efficiency toward IAAld compared with AldA. 3. Disruption of aldC did not reduce IAA accumulation in DC3000 cultures, indicating it is not a substantive contributor to IAA biosynthesis under the conditions tested. 4. Structural modeling suggested substrate-pocket features likely constrain IAAld use.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Bacterial culture supernatant in assay context
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: IAA acts as a virulence-associated auxin in this pathosystem, but aldC itself was not shown to materially control that function.
